title: Why do people say Shinji is selfish split
display_title: Why do people say Shinji is selfish split
wikitext:
[]: Why do people say Shinji is selfish? [split]             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Sun Apr 06, 2014 12:43 pm [Split from "Why do people say Shinji is a wimp?" - Monk.] Bagheera wrote: Second, what does she have to do with it? The post you're replying to was talking about his interaction with Misato after Kaji died.

[]:             Quote Post by sulla » Sun Apr 06, 2014 12:44 pm Bagheera wrote: First, she's not rotten, just an average kid who's traumatized as fuck. Second, what does she have to do with it? The post you're replying to was talking about his interaction with Misato after Kaji died. Generally agreed, yeah.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sun Apr 06, 2014 1:13 pm sulla wrote: I think people expect too much of a passive teenager.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Mon Apr 07, 2014 3:11 am xanderkh wrote: While I will acknowledge that Shinji is indeed one of the strongest and bravest characters I know, there's a big part of him that we unfortunately have to acknowledge and be critical of: His Selfishness.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Apr 07, 2014 4:30 am Monk Ed wrote: I've never really understood this business about Shinji being selfish. Episode 17 went out of its way to make a point that Shinji is actually a pretty sweet and considerate guy. Episodes 18 and 19 took it even further: In episode 18, Shinji demonstrates that he'd rather die than kill someone to save his own skin ( without even knowing who it was! ); and then in episode 19, Shinji demonstrates that he also wouldn't just sit by and let the world burn. And of course, let's not forget the many episodes where Shinji risks his life to save others.

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Mon Apr 07, 2014 4:38 am Bagheera wrote: No, Xander's right. Shinji doesn't refuse to kill someone for their sake in 18, he refuses because he doesn't want to be responsible. He doesn't care one whit that his chosen course will damn not only that person, but everyone else on the planet -- he's only concerned with the fact that he will be responsible.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Mon Apr 07, 2014 4:41 am @ Bagheera : I don't think that Shinji flat out didn't cared that his chosen course would damn the person in front of him (Toji in episode 18, Kaworu in 24) and also everyone else on the planet, but more that he simply didn't thought about it, as you said, he's a teenager with severe lack of experience in socializing. From here comes his inability to see the bigger picture and the consequences of his actions for everyone who isn't himself or occasionally the guy directly in front of him. Which is a normal behavior even for normal teenagers, except that the situations Shinji ends into have mankind's survival at stake, which means that he should had learned to grow-up ASAP to adapt to these circumstances, unfortunately there a limit of how much someone can adapt in so little time with so much stress, even more for a boy as Shinji with so many issues and who hate changes. As always, Rebuid was much more direct on it with Shinji grabbing the spears and later flat out told to us via Asuka's lecture when she found him prostrated in his entry plug.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Apr 07, 2014 5:03 am ElMariachi wrote: @ Bagheera : I don't think that Shinji flat out didn't cared that his chosen course would damn the person in front of him (Toji in episode 18, Kaworu in 24) and also everyone else on the planet, but more that he simply didn't thought about it, as you said, he's a teenager with severe lack of experience in socializing. From here comes his inability to see the bigger picture and the consequences of his actions for everyone who isn't himself or occasionally the guy directly in front of himself. Which is a normal behavior even for normal teenagers, except that the situations Shinji ends into have mankind's survival at stake, which means that he should had learned to grow-up ASAP to adapt to these circumstances, unfortunately there a limit of how much someone can adapt in so little time with so much stress, even more for a boy as Shinji with so many issues and who hate changes.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Mon Apr 07, 2014 5:05 am Bagheera wrote: No, Xander's right. (snip)

[]:             Quote Post by sulla » Mon Apr 07, 2014 5:49 am I would rather deal with someone who has avoidance behavioural issues, than someone who has cluster type be, narcissistic issues. I think parental abandonment can really mess a lot of people up. I think Shinji dealt with the hand he had been played very well. Lots of people would be doing far worse. I've noticed Americans tend to hate Shinji, I personally believe that America has hyper macho aspects to it's culture.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Apr 07, 2014 7:17 am sulla wrote: I would rather deal with someone who has avoidance behavioural issues, than someone who has cluster type be, narcissistic issues.

[]:             Quote Post by sulla » Mon Apr 07, 2014 9:48 am Topic is about people perceive Shinji. Comparing Shinji to other characters is not derailment.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Apr 07, 2014 10:07 am sulla wrote: Topic is about people perceive Shinji. Comparing Shinji to other characters is not derailment.

[]:             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Mon Apr 07, 2014 10:31 am @MonkEd: Oh, don't worry Monk Ed, there are plenty of moments where Shinji is selfless, otherwise he wouldn't have made the choice to bring people and the world back, which is probably the most selfless act I have seen. :) It's just that Gendo's abandonment completely fucked up any sort of natural social growth that Shinji was supposed to have, especially when he was just sent to his sensei (or uncle, whatever) to just "exist". Because of his abandonment, it created this huge mentality of self-blame for his problems, that he was the root of everything. And because of this, he became stuck in a level of Moral Thinking, known as "Preconventional Morality". We just studied this in Psychology again, but because he was stuck in this level of thinking, most of it is self-interest first (You obey the rules to avoid punishment or gain concrete rewards), because Shinji couldn't make any real connections with anyone during his life. And because he was stuck in a barely social-upholding environment with no form of real social help, where he just "existed" for 10 years, 10 YEARS, it's no wonder he's like that. He has the potential to be better and much, much stronger. It's just this part of his personality is a real turn-off for some people, in the West apparently (which is weird considering how "Individualistic" of a society we are compared to the "Communal" Japanese.)

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Mon Apr 07, 2014 12:38 pm ^ Decisiveness is a virtue in the West (often more than 'right action'). A decisive character is generally seen as a strong character (they are the Agent that Moves, as opposed to being Moved). I can't speak for Japan (I would assume this would be the same in Japan, as decisiveness is an Alpha trait that seems to exist regardless of culture). Personally, I don't think of Shinji as being one-hundred percent selfish all the time, though he is a certainly selfish character. All of the characters (especially the Children) display inward-focused elements that are very revealing of their characters (such as Asuka's self-loathing combined with highly advanced narcissism, among other things...). If anything, selfishness is a central theme to Shinji's character (among his many struggles is the struggle of fulfilling his own desires, or subduing them for the world's, and if this is a 'choice,' or something he does simply because he is told to).

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Apr 07, 2014 1:44 pm xanderkh wrote: @MonkEd: Oh, don't worry Monk Ed, there are plenty of moments where Shinji is selfless, otherwise he wouldn't have made the choice to bring people and the world back, which is probably the most selfless act I have seen. :)

[]:             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Mon Apr 07, 2014 3:06 pm Mr. Tines wrote: As he was voting to re-establish his own distinct self-hood first and foremost, and be damned to the consequences to others I would call it, at the very least, self-centered.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon Apr 07, 2014 4:21 pm xanderkh wrote: And, in your opinion, what would be the better option? To let humanity just exist as one singular entity, always at peace, but never changing, never moving forward; or have the world reborn and have everyone who's died before come back and help Rebuild a better world, despite all the pain?

[]:             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Mon Apr 07, 2014 5:00 pm NemZ wrote: 1) As long as he was in the center of things he had options. We'll never know what might have been possible because he didn't think to ask before calling it quits in the stupid belief that he could go back to the world just as it was before.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Apr 07, 2014 5:20 pm You're more or less on the money there, Xander, but Shinji's decision was still selfish: he preferred individuality to a lack of self free from pain. He made that choice for himself, not anyone else. We can give many reasons for why it was the right choice, but it was still inherently selfish when all is said and done. Which is fine in this case, but still.
